metadata.author: LivingPatch Contributors
metadata.description: American Beech tree, Beech aphids, and Harvester butterfly with their interconnected ecological relationships

species.functional_description: Medium to large deciduous tree with smooth gray bark and lance-shaped leaves. Produces edible triangular nuts (beechnuts) in hard spiny husks every 2-3 years (mast years). Critical food source for wildlife in fall and winter. Keystone species supporting diverse arthropod fauna. Long-lived (300+ years possible). Susceptible to beech bark disease and browsing pressure.

life_stages.name: Seedling
life_stages.description: Shade-tolerant seedlings in forest understory; slow initial growth

life_stages.name: Sapling
life_stages.description: Young tree competing in understory; can persist 20+ years before reaching canopy

life_stages.name: Mature
life_stages.description: Full-grown canopy tree; 60-80+ feet tall with broad crown

life_stages.name: Fruiting (Mast Year)
life_stages.description: Heavy nut production every 2-3 years; spiny husks open to reveal triangular nuts

life_stages.name: Dormant
life_stages.description: Leafless in winter; bare smooth gray bark characteristic

species.keystone_description: Keystone mast-producing tree supporting diverse wildlife in fall/winter including birds, mammals, and arthropods. Provides critical food when other resources scarce. Also key host for numerous insect species including aphids, scale insects, and defoliating insects. Long lifespan and structural complexity support diverse forest community.

species.functional_description: Small soft-bodied insects that form colonies on beech leaves and twigs, feeding on plant sap. Produce honeydew (sugary excretion) that attracts ants and other insects. Woolly-aphid forms have waxy white coating. High populations can reduce tree vigor but rarely cause severe damage. Aphids are primary food source for Harvester butterfly larvae through their relationship with attending ants.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Overwintering eggs laid on bark; hatch in spring

life_stages.name: Nymph
life_stages.description: Wingless juvenile stages; feed on sap in colonies

life_stages.name: Adult (Wingless)
life_stages.description: Parthenogenetic females producing clones; dominant form

life_stages.name: Winged Form
life_stages.description: Winged morphs produced in response to crowding; facilitate dispersal

species.functional_description: Unique butterfly — only North American butterfly species whose larvae feed on ants (specifically ants that tend aphid colonies). Small butterfly (wingspan 1.25-1.5 inches) with orange-brown wings. Adults feed on honeydew and nectar. Larvae hide among ant colonies and feed on worker ants while producing chemicals that mask them from ant detection. Critical predator controlling aphid populations through removal of tending ants.

life_stages.name: Egg
life_stages.description: Laid directly in or near ant-attended aphid colonies

life_stages.name: Caterpillar
life_stages.description: Cryptic slug-like larvae; feeds on ants; hides in colony; produces deceptive chemicals

life_stages.name: Chrysalis
life_stages.description: Forms in protected location among ants or vegetation

life_stages.name: Adult
life_stages.description: Orange-brown butterfly; feeds on honeydew and nectar near ant colonies

symbiosis.notes: Beech aphids feed on beech tree sap, causing minor damage. Mostly kept in check by natural enemies and weather. Honeydew production attracts ants.

Harvester butterfly larvae feed on ants that tend beech aphids. By removing protective ant attendants, larvae indirectly control aphid populations and disrupt ant-aphid mutualism. Critical regulation of aphid colonies.

relations.notes: Three-way ecological interaction: Beech tree hosts aphids; ants protect aphids (mutualism); Harvester butterfly larvae prey on ants, disrupting the ant-aphid relationship and controlling aphid populations.
